title: The Terminal That Shaped Vim — {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l}, {key:Esc}, and Why
subtitle: Why {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l}, why {key:Esc} sits where {key:Tab} is now, why colon means "command"
summary: Almost every weird Vim convention is the residue of a 1976 hardware terminal: the Lear Siegler ADM-3A. Knowing the keyboard explains the editor.

blocks.text: When Bill Joy wrote `vi` at UC Berkeley in 1976, the canonical "smart terminal" on campus was the Lear Siegler ADM-3A — a serial terminal, half desk-sized, that displayed 24 lines of 80 characters of green phosphor. It cost about $1,000 (roughly $5,000 in 2025 dollars). For three years it was Joy's keyboard, and almost every weird thing about Vim is a fingerprint of its layout — most famously the {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l} arrows and the placement of {key:Esc} right where modern keyboards put {key:Tab}.

blocks.caption: The Lear Siegler ADM-3A keyboard. Note the arrows printed on {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l}, the {key:Esc} key directly above {key:A} where {key:Tab} sits today, and {key:Ctrl} occupying the modern Caps-Lock position.

blocks.text: Some of what was special about the ADM-3A keyboard:

rows: Arrow keys
Printed on the {key:h}, {key:j}, {key:k}, {key:l} keys (no dedicated arrows)
{key:hjkl} for cursor movement
Esc key
Where {key:Tab} is on a modern keyboard — easy to reach
{key:Esc} is the universal way out of any mode
Caps Lock
Did not exist; the key in that position was Ctrl
{key:Ctrl-} chords are heavily used because Ctrl was always under your pinky
The {key::} key
Required Shift (it lives on the {key:;} key)
Ex commands feel like a deliberate gear-shift because typing {key::} was a tiny extra effort
No backtick
{key:`} key was missing on early ADM-3A revisions; mark notation used {key:'} only
{key:'a} (line of mark) is older than {key:`a} (exact column)
{key:~} key
Sat between {key:Esc} and {key:1} — used as the literal home directory glyph
Vim ended up reusing {key:~} for case-toggling because the keycap was easy to find

blocks.text: The {key:hjkl}-as-arrows decision was the original ADM-3A *hardware* designer's, not Joy's. Lear Siegler had stamped tiny arrows onto the keycaps of {key:h} (left), {key:j} (down), {key:k} (up), and {key:l} (right) so that users of `vi`-style or other modal editors could navigate without dedicated arrow keys. Joy looked at the keyboard he had and used what was already labeled. There was no abstract design judgement: he just used the arrows that were physically present.

Fifty years later, on a keyboard with proper arrow keys, {key:hjkl} still wins because it does not require leaving the home row. That is a real ergonomic benefit, but it is downstream of an accident: Joy could have picked any four keys, but those four were the ones with arrows printed on them. Once you know that, {key:hjkl} stops feeling like a design choice and starts feeling like an artifact.

If you ever wondered why you should leave the arrow keys alone in Vim and use {key:hjkl} instead, the answer is: not because the design is sacred, but because every Vim user expects {key:hjkl}, and tutorials and plugins assume it. The advantage is real (home-row movement), and the network effect makes it permanent.
Why Esc Is the Universal Exit

On the ADM-3A, the Esc key sat where a modern keyboard's {key:Tab} key sits — directly above {key:A}, easily hit with the left pinky. It was *the* meta-key for any program that needed an out-of-band signal. Bill Joy wired `vi` to use it as the universal "return to a known state" key, and we have lived with that decision ever since.

Modern keyboards put {key:Esc} on the top row, far from the home row, which is why so many Vim users remap {key:Caps Lock} to {key:Esc} (or {key:Ctrl}) — they are restoring the ADM-3A's ergonomics on hardware that has drifted away from them.

blocks.title: The Lineage of the Colon
blocks.text:
`vi` was originally a *visual* mode of `ex`, an extended version of `ed`. When you typed {key::}, `vi` shifted into ex's command-line interface — the line-editor underneath the screen interface — to accept ex commands like `:w`, `:q`, `:s/foo/bar/g`. The colon prefix is literally "now I am talking to ex." Vim and Neovim still embed an ex-mode interpreter; type {key:Q} (in Vim, not Neovim) to drop into pure *ex mode* and you'll see `vi`'s parent system underneath.

This is why ex commands have such a wildly different design from normal-mode commands. They are a different language, descended from `ed` (1969). `vi`/Vim/Neovim speak both languages and put a colon between them so you always know which one you're talking to.
Things You Can Stop Wondering About

rows: Use {key:hjkl} instead of arrows?
Arrow keys were on hjkl on Joy's terminal
Treat {key:Esc} as so important?
Esc sat in the comfortable spot Tab now occupies
Make ex commands need {key::} to start?
Vi was a visual layer over a separate command-line editor (ex)
Distinguish {key:'a} from {key:`a}?
Early ADM-3A keyboards lacked the backtick, so `vi` shipped {key:'} first
Use {key:Ctrl} as the canonical chord prefix?
Ctrl was where Caps Lock is now — easy to chord
Have so many keys that look meaningless ({key:Z}{key:Z}, {key:Z}{key:Q})?
Joy used pairs of identical keystrokes for emergency commands so muscle memory wouldn't accidentally trigger them

blocks.text: None of this is design. All of it is the editor wearing the shape of the keyboard it was born on. Once you see that, the apparent strangeness becomes legible.
